description: Case description and objective template pools. Keyed by case_type.complexity where complexity is 1, 2, or 3. Placeholders: $client, $subject, $victim, $pronoun (he/she), $pronounCap (He/She), $pron (he/she lowercase).

MISSING_PERSON.summary: A missing-person case requires a client who reports the disappearance, the missing subject, at least one witness, a last-known contact, and a neighbour. Higher complexity adds additional suspects.

INFIDELITY.summary: An infidelity case involves a client suspecting their partner, the subject, a third party, a mutual acquaintance, and a witness. Additional suspects appear at higher complexity.

THEFT.summary: A theft case requires a victim client, a primary suspect, a witness, an insurance adjuster, and a fence or dealer. Additional suspects are introduced at higher complexity.

FRAUD.summary: A fraud case involves a victim client, a perpetrator, an accountant, a business associate, and an insider witness. Higher complexity adds more suspects to the mix.

BLACKMAIL.summary: A blackmail case requires a victim client, the blackmailer, a witness, an intermediary, and a confidant. Additional suspects appear at higher complexity.

MURDER.summary: A murder case is the most complex type, requiring a client, a primary suspect, the victim, a key witness, the victim's associate, and a police contact. This is the only case type with a dead NPC. Additional suspects appear at higher complexity.

STALKING.summary: A stalking case involves a victim client, the stalker, a neighbour witness, an ex-partner, and a friend of the client. Additional suspects appear at higher complexity.

CORPORATE_ESPIONAGE.summary: A corporate espionage case requires an employer client, the suspected leak, a rival contact, a trusted colleague, and an IT specialist. Additional suspects appear at higher complexity.

facts: $subject was last seen near the east dock at approximately 11 p.m. on the night of the disappearance.
A half-eaten meal and an unlocked front door were found at $subject's residence.

leads.hint: A dock worker may have seen $subject on the night in question.
leads.description: A dock worker at the east pier recalls seeing someone matching $subject's description waiting near the loading bay around 11 p.m. They appeared to be waiting for someone who arrived in a dark-coloured vehicle.

leads.hint: $subject's home shows signs of a sudden departure — a closer look may reveal why.
leads.description: The scene at $subject's flat — an unlocked door, an uneaten meal — suggests $pron left in a hurry. A closer inspection reveals a torn envelope behind the sofa with a partial return address.

facts: $subject withdrew £2,000 in cash from an ATM two days before vanishing.
A neighbour heard raised voices coming from $subject's flat the night before the disappearance.

leads.hint: Recent financial activity suggests the disappearance may have been planned — or coerced.
leads.description: Bank records show $subject withdrew £2,000 in cash in a single transaction at an ATM near the train station. The withdrawal was unusual — $subject typically uses card payments.

leads.hint: Neighbours may have overheard something relevant the night before $subject vanished.
leads.description: The upstairs neighbour heard two voices arguing loudly around 10:30 p.m. One voice was $subject's; the other was unfamiliar. The argument ended with a door slamming.

facts: $subject's mobile phone was last active near a parking structure on Oak Street.
$subject's car is still in the driveway, untouched since the disappearance.

leads.hint: The area around $subject's phone's last known location has not been fully searched.
leads.description: Cell tower data places $subject's phone near the multi-storey car park on Oak Street at 11:47 p.m. The phone went dark after that. The car park has limited CCTV but adjacent businesses may have exterior cameras.

leads.hint: If $subject's car is still at home, $pron left by other means — someone may have seen how.
leads.description: The car sits undisturbed in the driveway with no sign of tampering. A taxi company operating in the area has records of a pickup from a nearby street at 11:15 p.m. on the night in question.

facts: $subject sent a cryptic text message — just a location pin — from $pron phone before it went silent.
The location pin points to an industrial estate on the outskirts of town.

leads.hint: The location $subject sent before going silent warrants investigation.
leads.description: The GPS pin embedded in the text message points to Unit 7 of the Millbrook Industrial Estate. The unit is listed as vacant but the roller shutter shows signs of recent use.

leads.hint: $subject's phone records may reveal who $pron was communicating with before the message was sent.
leads.description: Phone records show $subject exchanged six calls with an unregistered number in the two hours before sending the pin. The calls were brief — none longer than 40 seconds.

facts: A CCTV camera near $subject's home recorded an unfamiliar dark saloon parked outside for three hours on the night of the disappearance.
$subject asked a colleague about passport replacement procedures the week before vanishing.

leads.hint: CCTV from the area may show who visited $subject the night $pron disappeared.
leads.description: A camera mounted on a neighbouring business captured a dark saloon (partial plate visible) arriving at 9:14 p.m. and leaving at 12:22 a.m. A figure is seen entering the passenger side before it departs.

leads.hint: A colleague may know why $subject was asking about travel documents.
leads.description: A co-worker in HR confirms $subject asked about emergency passport procedures, saying $pron had 'lost' the original. $subject seemed anxious and asked the colleague not to mention the enquiry.

facts: $subject missed a family gathering without explanation — entirely out of character.
A friend says $subject mentioned being 'in some kind of trouble' but refused to elaborate.

leads.hint: Family members may have noticed warning signs before $subject disappeared.
leads.description: The family confirms $subject had been withdrawn for several weeks. $subject cancelled two planned visits with vague excuses and stopped answering calls from certain numbers.

leads.hint: The friend $subject confided in may know more than they initially let on.
leads.description: $subject's friend recalls a conversation three days before the disappearance. $subject said someone was 'making threats' but wouldn't name them. The friend noticed $subject checking over $pron shoulder repeatedly.

facts: $subject left a note saying $pron 'needed space', but the handwriting appears shaky and unnatural.
Forensic analysis of the note's ink suggests it was written at least two weeks before it was supposedly left.

leads.hint: The note $subject allegedly left may not be genuine.
leads.description: Handwriting analysis indicates the note was written under duress or by someone attempting to imitate $subject's hand. Several letter formations differ from $subject's known samples.

leads.hint: Examining the paper and ink may reveal when the note was actually written.
leads.description: Ink oxidation analysis dates the note to approximately two weeks before the disappearance. This contradicts the claim it was left the day $subject vanished, suggesting it was prepared in advance — possibly under coercion.

facts: $subject's online accounts show no activity since the morning of the disappearance.
A delivery driver saw $subject leaving the house with a small bag around 6 a.m.

leads.hint: $subject's digital footprint stopped abruptly — checking online accounts may reveal why.
leads.description: All social media, email, and banking apps show last activity between 5:30 and 6:15 a.m. on the day of the disappearance. A draft email was started but never sent — the subject line reads 'If anything happens'.

leads.hint: Someone saw $subject leaving early that morning — they may recall useful details.
leads.description: The regular delivery driver saw $subject step out of the front door at approximately 6:10 a.m. carrying a small sports bag. $subject looked around nervously before walking quickly toward the main road.

facts: A second phone — a prepaid handset — was found hidden in $subject's desk drawer.
The prepaid phone contains three text messages from the same unregistered number, all with map coordinates.

leads.hint: A hidden phone in $subject's desk may contain clues about $pron movements.
leads.description: The prepaid handset is a basic model purchased with cash. It contains only incoming messages — $subject never replied. The phone's call log is empty.

leads.hint: The map coordinates in the messages may lead to locations $subject visited — or was told to visit.
leads.description: The three sets of coordinates correspond to: a layby on the A34, a disused railway building, and a residential address in a neighbouring town. None are obviously connected to $subject's known routines.

facts: $subject was involved in a minor traffic incident two days before the disappearance; the other driver's details were never exchanged.
The incident occurred near an address $subject has no known connection to.

leads.hint: The traffic incident shortly before the disappearance may not have been accidental.
leads.description: Police records show $subject reported a minor rear-end collision at a junction near Ashford Road. The other vehicle left before details were exchanged. Dash-cam footage from a passing bus may exist.

leads.hint: The location of the incident is not somewhere $subject would normally be.
leads.description: The junction is in a quiet residential area four miles from $subject's home and workplace. The only notable address nearby is a rented flat — the tenant moved in six weeks ago under a name that doesn't check out.

facts: A locksmith was called to $subject's property the day before the disappearance.
The locksmith's invoice was paid in cash by someone other than $subject.

leads.hint: The locksmith who visited $subject's home may have useful information.
leads.description: The locksmith confirms being called to change the front-door lock. The person who met him at the property was not $subject — they gave a false name and paid £120 in cash.

leads.hint: Someone else arranged and paid for work at $subject's home — finding out who may be critical.
leads.description: The locksmith describes the payer as a man in his 40s, medium build, wearing a dark jacket. The description doesn't match anyone in $subject's known circle.

facts: $subject's gym membership card was used at a facility 30 miles from home on the morning of the disappearance.
The gym's sign-in system recorded an entry at 7:02 a.m. but no exit.

leads.hint: $subject's gym card was used far from home on the day $pron vanished.
leads.description: The gym is a large 24-hour facility near a motorway junction. Staff don't recall seeing $subject. The car park has CCTV that recycles every 72 hours — the footage may still be recoverable.

leads.hint: The gym has no record of $subject leaving — $pron may have exited through an alternative route.
leads.description: The facility has a fire exit at the rear that opens onto a service road. The exit is not covered by cameras. A delivery driver who uses the same road each morning may have noticed something.

facts: $subject has been working late three nights a week, but the extra hours do not appear on $pron pay slips.
A restaurant receipt for two was found in $subject's jacket — for a restaurant $client has never visited with $subject.

leads.hint: $subject's work schedule and actual whereabouts may not match.

facts: A voice message left by the blackmailer has been partially cleaned up by audio analysis.
The voice profile is consistent with $subject's despite attempts at distortion.

leads.hint: Audio forensics on the voice messages may identify the blackmailer.
leads.description: The blackmailer left a voicemail using a voice distortion app. Audio engineers have partially reversed the effect, revealing speech patterns and an accent consistent with $subject's known voice samples.

leads.hint: The voice distortion app used may be traceable through $subject's device.
leads.description: The distortion effect matches a specific app available on both iOS and Android. If $subject's phone or purchase history shows the app was downloaded, it would be strong circumstantial evidence.

facts: Traces of a sedative not consistent with $victim's prescription were found on a glass near the scene.
$subject's fingerprints were found on the glass alongside the sedative traces.

leads.hint: Forensic analysis of objects at the scene may have been incomplete.
leads.description: The glass contained residue of a benzodiazepine — a sedative not prescribed to $victim. The glass also carried two sets of prints: $victim's and $subject's. $subject has not explained why $pron prints are on the glass.

leads.hint: $subject may have had access to the sedative found at the scene.
leads.description: A search of pharmacy records shows a prescription for the same benzodiazepine was filled at a chemist near $subject's home two weeks before $victim's death. The prescription was issued under a name that doesn't check out.

facts: $subject altered $pron alibi between the first and second police interviews.
A witness can confirm the discrepancy.

leads.hint: Inconsistencies in the official account may surface when speaking to those who were present.
leads.description: In the first interview, $subject claimed to be at a restaurant until 10 p.m. In the second, $pron said $pron left at 9:15. The restaurant's booking system confirms a table in $subject's name with a bill paid at 9:22.

leads.hint: Re-examining police interview transcripts may reveal the alibi change.
leads.description: The original police transcripts are available through a freedom-of-information request. Comparing the two statements side by side makes the discrepancy unmistakable and raises questions about what $subject was doing between 9:22 and 10 p.m.

facts: $subject was observed watching $victim's residence on three evenings in the week before the death.
The observation was from a parked car with the engine running.

leads.hint: Surveillance of $subject's behaviour patterns may reveal prior knowledge of $victim's movements.
leads.description: A neighbour of $victim noticed a car parked across the street on three consecutive evenings. On the third evening, they noted the registration — it matches $subject's vehicle.

leads.hint: $victim's neighbours may have noticed someone watching the property.
leads.description: The neighbour is willing to provide a statement and can describe the vehicle and its occupant. They also recall the car leaving abruptly when $victim closed the curtains.

facts: $victim sent a message naming $subject hours before $pron death.
The message was deleted remotely from $victim's phone.

leads.hint: Digital records from the days surrounding the incident may be critical.
leads.description: $victim sent a text to a friend at 6:47 p.m. reading: 'If anything happens to me, look at $subject.' The message was deleted from $victim's phone remotely at 11:30 p.m. — after the estimated time of death.

leads.hint: The friend who received $victim's message may still have it.
leads.description: The friend's phone still contains the received message. They didn't understand it at the time but now recognise its significance. They are willing to share the message and provide a statement.

facts: Independent forensic analysis has narrowed the time of death to a window that contradicts $subject's alibi.
The original coroner's report placed the time of death two hours earlier.

leads.hint: Commissioning an independent forensic review could narrow the time of death and expose alibi gaps.
leads.description: An independent pathologist has reviewed the original findings and concluded the time of death was between 9:30 and 10:15 p.m. — a window during which $subject claims to have been driving home alone with no witnesses.

leads.hint: The discrepancy between the original and revised time of death may indicate pressure on the initial investigation.
leads.description: The original coroner's report was completed in unusual haste. A colleague of the coroner has expressed concerns privately about the methodology used and may be willing to discuss them.

facts: $subject took out a substantial life insurance policy on $victim six months before the death.
$subject is the sole beneficiary.

leads.hint: Financial motives may emerge from insurance and inheritance records.
leads.description: A life insurance policy worth £250,000 was taken out on $victim with $subject named as the sole beneficiary. The policy was purchased through an online broker, and $victim's signature on the application may be forged.

leads.hint: The insurance broker who processed the policy may recall the circumstances of the application.
leads.description: The broker recalls the application was submitted online with minimal verification. The medical section was completed without a doctor's examination, which the broker now admits should have raised a flag.

facts: Soil samples from $subject's shoes match the soil type found at the location where $victim's body was discovered.
$subject claims never to have visited that location.

leads.hint: Trace evidence on $subject's clothing or footwear may place $pron at the crime scene.
leads.description: Soil analysis from $subject's walking boots shows a distinctive clay-and-chalk mixture consistent with the terrain at the discovery site. This soil type is uncommon and not found near $subject's home or workplace.

leads.hint: Geolocation data from $subject's phone may confirm whether $pron visited the area.
leads.description: Although $subject's phone was switched off during the critical hours, a brief reconnection at 2:17 a.m. pinged a cell tower serving the area where $victim was found.

facts: A witness heard $subject and $victim arguing violently two days before the death.
$subject was overheard saying 'you won't get away with this'.

leads.hint: Witnesses who were present during the argument may recall what was said.
leads.description: The argument took place in the car park of a pub. Two patrons heard raised voices. One recalls $subject grabbing $victim's arm. The other heard $subject say 'you won't get away with this' before $victim pulled free and left.

leads.hint: The pub's CCTV may have captured the confrontation.
leads.description: The pub's exterior camera covers part of the car park. Footage from the evening in question shows two figures in a heated exchange at approximately 8:45 p.m. The angle may be sufficient to confirm identities.

facts: $victim's will was recently changed to exclude $subject.
$subject learned of the change the week before $victim died.

leads.hint: Changes to $victim's will shortly before the death may point to a motive.
leads.description: The solicitor handling $victim's estate confirms the will was amended four weeks before the death. $subject was removed as a beneficiary and replaced by a charity. $subject was informed by letter.

leads.hint: $subject's reaction to being disinherited may have been witnessed by others.
leads.description: A friend of $subject recalls $pron being furious after receiving a letter from a solicitor. $subject said $victim had 'made a huge mistake' and that $pron 'wasn't going to let it stand'.

facts: A knife recovered from $subject's vehicle has been confirmed as consistent with the weapon used.
$subject claims the knife is a work tool and has always been in the van.

leads.hint: A weapon consistent with the injuries may be linked to $subject.
leads.description: A 15cm fixed-blade knife found under the driver's seat of $subject's van matches the wound profile described in the pathology report. Luminol testing detected traces of blood on the handle despite apparent cleaning.

leads.hint: DNA or blood traces on the weapon may connect it to $victim.
leads.description: Preliminary blood analysis from the knife handle returned a partial match to $victim's blood type. Full DNA profiling is pending but expected to confirm the match.

facts: $subject purchased a burner phone two days before $victim's death.
The burner phone was used to call $victim three times on the night of the killing.

leads.hint: A prepaid phone purchased by $subject shortly before the death may have been used to lure $victim.
leads.description: CCTV at a phone shop shows $subject purchasing a prepaid handset with cash. The phone's number was used to call $victim at 8:12, 9:03, and 9:41 p.m. on the night of the death. The final call lasted seven minutes.

leads.hint: The burner phone may still be in $subject's possession or have been disposed of nearby.
leads.description: The phone went inactive after the final call. Cell tower data from its last transmission places it near a waste bin behind a service station on the A12 — a route consistent with $subject's drive home.

facts: $subject searched online for 'how to make a death look accidental' in the weeks before $victim died.
The search history was deleted but recovered from cached data.

leads.hint: $subject's internet search history may contain incriminating queries.
leads.description: Browser cache analysis recovered search queries including 'undetectable poisons', 'how to stage an accident', and 'how long does DNA last outdoors'. The searches were made over a two-week period ending three days before $victim's death.

leads.hint: The device used for the searches may contain additional evidence.
leads.description: $subject's personal laptop contains a cleared browser history, but forensic data recovery has retrieved cached pages, bookmarks, and a downloaded PDF titled 'Forensic Countermeasures'. The laptop's MAC address matches the device used for the searches.

facts: $subject has photographed $client's home and daily routine over a six-week period.
A folder of printed images was found discarded near $subject's residence.

leads.hint: $subject's personal space may contain evidence of obsessive tracking.
leads.description: The discarded folder contains 47 photographs of $client's home, taken from different angles at different times of day. Some include notes on $client's departure and arrival times in $subject's handwriting.

leads.hint: Neighbours near where the photos were taken may have seen $subject in the area.
leads.description: A resident on $client's street recalls seeing someone parked in a silver car on multiple occasions, always facing $client's house. The description of the driver matches $subject.

facts: $subject created multiple fake online accounts to monitor and contact $client.
The accounts use variations of the same username pattern.

leads.hint: Tracing digital footprints may identify the source of online harassment.
leads.description: The fake accounts — @shadow_watcher, @shadow_w4tcher, @sh4dow_watcher — all follow $client and have sent unwanted messages. IP login data from two of the accounts traces to $subject's home broadband connection.

leads.hint: The pattern of online activity may reveal $subject's monitoring habits.
leads.description: The accounts engage with $client's posts within minutes of publication, suggesting real-time monitoring. Activity timestamps cluster around $subject's known work breaks and evening hours.

facts: A former partner of $subject filed a similar stalking complaint two years ago in another city.
The complaint was upheld and a restraining order was issued.

leads.hint: A background investigation may reveal a pattern of stalking behaviour.
leads.description: Police records from another jurisdiction show $subject was subject to a restraining order obtained by a former partner. The behaviour described — persistent surveillance, unwanted gifts, and intimidating messages — mirrors the current case.

leads.hint: The previous victim may be willing to share their experience.
leads.description: The former partner has been contacted through their solicitor and is willing to provide a statement. They describe an escalating pattern that began with online monitoring and progressed to in-person following.

facts: $client has received unwanted gifts left at $pron doorstep — always when $pron is out.
The gifts include items that reference private conversations $client had at home.

leads.hint: The timing of the gifts suggests $subject knows $client's schedule — surveillance of the area may catch $pron in the act.
leads.description: The gifts appear within 30 minutes of $client leaving home. Setting up a concealed camera or arranging for someone to watch the property during $client's next departure may capture $subject delivering one.

leads.hint: The content of the gifts suggests $client's private conversations are being overheard.
leads.description: One gift included a book that $client mentioned wanting during a phone call made from home. If the home is not bugged, $subject may be using a directional microphone or has access to $client's phone.

facts: $subject's vehicle has been spotted on $client's street at least twice a week for the past month.
ANPR data confirms the vehicle's presence on dates matching $client's reports.

leads.hint: Number-plate recognition data may confirm $subject's vehicle was in $client's area on the reported dates.
leads.description: ANPR cameras on the main road entering $client's estate recorded $subject's vehicle on 9 of the last 14 days. All readings fall within a two-hour window around $client's typical return-from-work time.

leads.hint: $client's neighbours may have dashcam footage showing $subject's vehicle.
leads.description: A neighbour with a front-facing dashcam has offered to check their recordings. Preliminary review of two dates shows a vehicle matching $subject's parked 50 metres from $client's front door.

facts: $subject has been sending letters to $client's workplace, causing distress and professional embarrassment.
The letters contain personal information that should not be publicly known.

leads.hint: The letters may carry forensic evidence linking them to $subject.
leads.description: The letters are handwritten on distinctive textured paper. Envelope adhesive may carry DNA. The postmark is from $subject's local post office. Fingerprint analysis has not yet been attempted.

leads.hint: Co-workers who received or handled the letters may recall delivery details.
leads.description: The receptionist at $client's office confirms the letters arrive by Royal Mail, always on a Monday morning. One letter was hand-delivered — the receptionist may have seen the person who dropped it off.

facts: $client found a GPS tracking device attached to the underside of $pron car.
The device model is a commercially available tracker that logs location history.

leads.hint: The tracking device may carry purchase records or serial numbers that identify the buyer.
leads.description: The GPS tracker is a commercial model sold online. The serial number may be traceable to a purchase order. $subject's delivery records or credit card history could show the purchase.

leads.hint: The device's stored location data may reveal where $subject has been collecting the tracking information.
leads.description: The tracker uploads location data to a cloud account. Gaining access to the account (with appropriate legal authority) would reveal when the device was placed and how the data has been used.

facts: $subject has been approaching $client's friends and family, asking invasive questions about $client's life.
Three people have independently reported feeling uncomfortable about $subject's questioning.

leads.hint: $client's friends and family may have been approached by $subject seeking personal information.
leads.description: Three of $client's contacts — a sister, a colleague, and a gym partner — report being approached by $subject in the past month. In each case, $subject steered conversation toward $client's schedule, relationships, and habits.

leads.hint: The pattern of $subject's approaches may establish a deliberate information-gathering campaign.
leads.description: Mapping the contacts $subject has approached, the questions asked, and the dates of each encounter reveals a systematic effort to build a detailed profile of $client's daily life.

facts: $client's email account was accessed from an unrecognised device on three occasions.
The login IP addresses trace to locations near $subject's home and workplace.

leads.hint: $client's email security logs may reveal unauthorised access.
leads.description: Login records show three access events from devices not recognised by $client. The sessions lasted between 5 and 20 minutes and occurred during hours when $client was at work.

leads.hint: The IP addresses used for the unauthorised logins may be traceable to $subject.
leads.description: Two of the three IP addresses resolve to a residential broadband connection in $subject's postcode area. The third is a public Wi-Fi network at a café 200 metres from $subject's workplace.

facts: $subject left a voicemail on $client's phone that was initially calm but became increasingly threatening.
The voicemail was left at 2:30 a.m.

leads.hint: The voicemail left by $subject may constitute evidence of threatening behaviour.
leads.description: The 4-minute voicemail begins with $subject saying $pron 'just wants to talk' but escalates to threats about 'consequences' if $client continues to ignore $pron. The tone becomes aggressive in the final minute.

leads.hint: Phone records may show a pattern of unwanted contact attempts.
leads.description: Call logs show $subject attempted to call $client 23 times in the past week, predominantly between midnight and 4 a.m. Text messages sent from the same number include demands to meet.

facts: $subject was seen waiting outside $client's child's school.
The school has no record of $subject being authorised to collect any child.

leads.hint: School staff may have noticed $subject's presence near the school.
leads.description: A teacher on playground duty noticed an adult matching $subject's description standing at the school gate on two afternoons. They were not collecting a child and left when the teacher approached.

leads.hint: School CCTV may have captured $subject near the premises.
leads.description: The school's external cameras cover the main gate and car park. Footage from the dates in question may show $subject's presence and vehicle, establishing a pattern of attending the school.

facts: $client's social media accounts have been flooded with anonymous comments and messages.
The language and timing of the messages suggest a single person operating multiple accounts.

leads.hint: The anonymous accounts may share digital fingerprints that link them to $subject.
leads.description: Analysis of the accounts shows they were all created within a 48-hour window, use the same email provider, and exhibit identical posting patterns. Two accounts share a profile image that was cropped from a photo on $subject's real social media.

leads.hint: Platform abuse reports may trigger account disclosure that identifies $subject.
leads.description: Formal reports to the social media platform may compel disclosure of the accounts' registration data. If the email addresses or phone numbers used match $subject's known contact details, the link is established.

facts: $subject accessed confidential project files outside normal working hours on at least twelve occasions.
The access logs show downloads of sensitive pricing and strategy documents.

leads.hint: Internal access logs may show irregular system activity by $subject.
leads.description: IT security logs reveal $subject accessed the shared drive containing project files between 10 p.m. and 2 a.m. on 12 occasions over three months. Each session involved downloading documents flagged as 'confidential' or 'restricted'.

leads.hint: $subject may have transferred the downloaded files to an external device.
leads.description: USB audit logs show a removable storage device was connected to $subject's workstation during several of the late-night sessions. The device ID does not match any company-issued equipment.

facts: A colleague saw $subject photographing a whiteboard during a closed strategy session.
$subject was not authorised to attend the session.

leads.hint: Other staff members may have witnessed $subject acting suspiciously.
leads.description: The colleague noticed $subject standing near the boardroom door with a phone raised. When challenged, $subject claimed to be checking a text message. The colleague is willing to provide a formal statement.

leads.hint: $subject's phone gallery may contain images of confidential material.
leads.description: If the phone can be examined, the gallery and recently deleted folder may contain photographs of the whiteboard content, meeting slides, or other restricted material.

facts: $subject meets a contact from a rival firm monthly at a location away from both offices.
The meetings take place at a pub in a neighbouring town.

leads.hint: Regular off-site meetings with a competitor employee would confirm the leak.
leads.description: Surveillance over two months has identified a pattern: $subject and the contact meet at the Crown and Anchor pub on the third Thursday of each month, arriving separately and sitting in the back room.

leads.hint: The identity of $subject's contact at the rival firm may be established through their records.
leads.description: The contact's vehicle registration was noted during surveillance. It is registered to an employee of the rival firm who works in their business development division.

facts: $subject emailed proprietary documents to a personal email address.
The personal address uses a pseudonym but is linked to $subject's phone number.

leads.hint: Email server logs may show data being sent to external addresses.
leads.description: The company's outbound email filter flagged three messages from $subject's work account to an external Gmail address. The messages contained attachments totalling 45 MB — including product roadmaps and client pricing.

leads.hint: The external email account may be traceable to $subject through associated recovery details.
leads.description: The Gmail account's recovery phone number matches $subject's personal mobile. The account name — 'greyconsulting' — does not correspond to any registered business.

facts: A USB device found in $subject's desk contains copies of trade-secret documents.
The documents include material $subject was not authorised to access.

leads.hint: A search of $subject's workspace may reveal undisclosed copies of sensitive material.
leads.description: The USB drive — found in a locked drawer — contains 23 files, including the company's five-year strategic plan, supplier contracts, and a client database. The files were copied over a period of four months.

leads.hint: File metadata on the USB drive may show when and how the documents were copied.
leads.description: Metadata timestamps show the files were copied during $subject's logged working hours. The drive's serial number may link it to a purchase made by $subject.

facts: The rival firm launched a competing product with features strikingly similar to a project $subject worked on.
The rival's product appeared just three months after $subject joined the project team.

leads.hint: The timeline of the rival's product launch may correlate with $subject's access to the project.
leads.description: The rival firm announced their competing product 14 weeks after $subject was added to the project team. The features mirror internal design documents that $subject had access to. The rival's patent application references specifications identical to the company's unpublished research.

leads.hint: Former employees of the rival firm may confirm that the leaked material was used.
leads.description: A recently departed employee of the rival firm has indicated willingness to discuss how the competing product was developed. They claim the project team received a 'briefing document' that appeared to originate from an external source.

facts: $subject received unexplained payments into a personal account from a source linked to the competitor.
The payments total £35,000 over six months.

leads.hint: $subject's personal finances may show payments from an unknown or suspicious source.
leads.description: Bank statements show six transfers of varying amounts (£4,000–£8,000) from a business account registered to a consultancy firm. The firm's only client is the rival company.

leads.hint: The consultancy firm making the payments may be a front for the competitor.
leads.description: The consultancy — 'Apex Advisory' — has no public profile, one director (based overseas), and a virtual office address. Its bank account receives regular funding from the rival company and pays out exclusively to $subject.

facts: $subject was seen entering the competitor's offices after hours.
Security footage from the competitor's building captured $subject's face.

leads.hint: $subject may have been physically present at the competitor's premises.
leads.description: External CCTV from the competitor's building shows $subject entering through a side door at 7:45 p.m. on a Tuesday. $subject was inside for approximately 90 minutes. A visitor pass was not logged.

leads.hint: The competitor's reception or security staff may recall $subject's visit.
leads.description: A security guard at the building recalls someone matching $subject's description being met at the side entrance by a member of staff. The guard did not log the visit because the staff member vouched for the visitor.

facts: $subject's home computer contains a folder of company documents marked 'CONFIDENTIAL'.
The documents include material from projects $subject was not assigned to.

leads.hint: $subject's personal devices may contain copies of company data.
leads.description: A trusted source reports seeing company-branded documents on $subject's home laptop during a social visit. The documents included project briefs and financial forecasts marked 'Internal — Confidential'.

leads.hint: Comparing the documents on $subject's computer to the company's version-control system could date the leak.
leads.description: If the files can be examined, their metadata and version numbers can be matched to specific revisions in the company's document management system, pinpointing when they were copied.

facts: $subject booked a conference room to make private phone calls to the competitor.
Call logs from the room's phone show regular contact with a number registered to the rival firm.

leads.hint: Conference room booking records and phone logs may reveal suspicious communication.
leads.description: $subject booked a small meeting room 15 times in two months, always for 30-minute slots. The room's phone was used to dial the same external number each time — a direct line at the competitor's head office.

leads.hint: IT may be able to recover recordings or transcripts of the calls.
leads.description: The company's phone system records outbound calls for quality and compliance purposes. If recordings exist for the calls made from the meeting room, they may contain direct evidence of information sharing.

facts: $subject has been using a personal cloud storage account to sync company files.
The cloud account is not company-sanctioned and bypasses IT security controls.

leads.hint: $subject may be using unsanctioned cloud services to exfiltrate data.
leads.description: Network monitoring detected $subject's workstation uploading large files to a personal Dropbox account. The uploads occurred during working hours and included files from the R&D shared drive.

leads.hint: The cloud account's sharing settings may show whether files were shared with external parties.
leads.description: If the Dropbox account can be accessed, its sharing history will show whether files were distributed to other accounts. Shared-link logs may reveal the email addresses of recipients.

facts: A draft email found in $subject's outbox contained an attachment of the company's client list.
The intended recipient's email domain belongs to the competitor.

leads.hint: $subject's email drafts may contain unsent messages with sensitive attachments.
leads.description: The draft was addressed to an individual at the rival firm and contained a spreadsheet of the company's top 200 clients, including contract values and renewal dates. The draft was created but apparently not sent — or was sent and re-saved as a draft.

leads.hint: The intended recipient at the rival firm may confirm whether they received the data.
leads.description: The recipient's LinkedIn profile identifies them as head of new-business at the competitor. If approached as part of a formal investigation, they may be compelled to disclose what they received.
